name: Thermus aquaticus
description: A heat-loving bacterium from Yellowstone's hot springs whose Taq enzyme revolutionized PCR technology, transforming genetics, forensics, and medical diagnostics worldwide.

name: Pyrolobus fumarii
location: Von Damm Hydrothermal Field, Mid-Cayman Rise
description: A deep-sea archaeon thriving at 113°C near the Von Damm hydrothermal vents on the Mid-Cayman Rise, holding one of the highest known temperature records for any living organism.

name: Halobacterium salinarum
description: A salt-loving archaeon thriving in Australia's Lake Tyrrell, harvesting sunlight through bacteriorhodopsin — a unique pigment-based energy system requiring no chlorophyll at all.

name: Colwellia psychrerythraea
location: Arctic Basin, North of Canadian Arctic Archipelago
description: A cold-loving bacterium from the Arctic Basin's icy marine sediments north of Canada that remains metabolically active below -10°C, modeling how life might survive on icy ocean worlds like Europa.

name: Acidithiobacillus ferrooxidans
description: An acid-loving bacterium from the Gobi Desert's mining regions that oxidizes iron and sulfur for energy, studied as a Mars analogue and used commercially in biomining operations across Central Asia.

name: Deinococcus radiodurans
description: Earth's most radiation-resistant bacterium, found in Chernobyl's exclusion zone, capable of reassembling its completely shattered genome within hours of lethal radiation exposure.

name: Chroococcidiopsis
description: A cyanobacterium living inside Atacama Desert rocks, using stone as a UV shield while photosynthesizing — considered the closest Earth analogue to potential life on Mars.

name: Halomonas campisalis
description: A salt- and alkali-loving bacterium from Kenya's Lake Bogoria, surviving at pH 10.5 where extreme alkalinity and high salinity would destroy almost any other cell.
